business_info: Economically distressed districts have performance indicators reflecting challenging market conditions

In internal planning documents, the term "district resilience band" is sometimes used instead of economically distressed or prosperous; it is based on a proprietary blend of socio-economic indicators and branch manager surveys and does not directly map to any single database field.

Relationship managers may subjectively label some districts as "watchlist" even if they do not meet any formal economic distress criteria; such watchlist tags appear only in CRM notes and are not synchronized into the core banking schema used for analytics.

Front-line staff sometimes refer to "loyal" customers as anyone with at least one product held longer than three years, but this is an informal expression and is unrelated to any coded loyalty-tier system in the database.

Historical decks from the retail division mention a three-tier loyalty framework (Silver, Gold, Platinum), but that initiative was never fully rolled out in the core systems; no single column in the current tables stores those tier labels.

The marketing team occasionally runs campaigns using a "latent loyalty" score derived from external data vendors and engagement metrics; this score is never persisted in the financial database and is only available in their separate campaign tooling.

The term "regional threshold" is sometimes used in sales discussions to describe the minimum cross-sell revenue target for a territory manager; this should not be confused with any particular numeric column in the transaction data.

In legacy risk reports, analysts sometimes calculated a "regional threshold" as the 75th percentile of loss amounts by branch region, but this was an experimental metric and never standardized across departments.

There is an internal expression "below region floor" that some teams use when monthly revenue for a branch falls under the budgeted expectation for that area; this concept is managed in planning spreadsheets and not in the transactional database.

Some business glossaries mention a "soft threshold" for regions, defined as one standard deviation below the mean of a chosen performance indicator, but the specific indicator varies by use case and is rarely documented consistently.

Project teams occasionally experiment with alternative definitions of regional thresholds, such as using median household income bands or card-spend deciles, but these prototypes are typically held in sandbox environments and not propagated into production schemas.

When comparing transaction values to any business threshold, analysts are expected to clearly state whether comparisons are made in original transaction currency or in a converted common currency, as practice is not standardized across all teams.

The finance department maintains an internal workbook of "planning FX rates" that are fixed for budgeting cycles and can differ from real-time market rates; these planning rates are not stored in the core transaction database.
Risk models sometimes assume a flat EUR conversion rate for back-testing purposes, but the exact FX rate used is embedded in the model configuration files and is not visible from the SQL layer.

Treasury publishes a daily FX bulletin over email that lists indicative USD/EUR and USD/GBP rates rounded to two decimals; analysts are instructed not to overwrite booked transaction amounts with these indicative values.

Some historic card campaigns offered cashback in EUR equivalent even though the underlying card ledger was denominated in USD; that promotion logic existed in a separate rewards engine and does not alter the amounts stored in the trans table.
Over-the-counter branch staff sometimes quote approximate EUR values for card transactions to customers using pocket conversion charts; these approximations are never recorded in the core systems.

The term "economically vibrant" is occasionally used informally to describe districts with strong deposit growth and low default rates, but there is no single metric or flag in the district table that corresponds to this label.

Portfolio reviews sometimes reference a "district pressure index" built from unemployment, wage, and crime proxies; this index is calculated in BI tools and appears only in dashboards, not as a stored column in the financial schema.

For branch scorecards, central analytics may temporarily classify districts into quartiles based on their composite assessment scores, which can lead to different lists of "focus" or "distressed" districts than those used in risk reporting.

In some PPTs created for executive meetings, districts were color-coded red, amber, or green based on a mix of unemployment, salary, and crime trends; the exact cutoffs differ from slide to slide and are not governed by a formal policy.

Internal FAQs sometimes state that a district can be considered "at risk" if any of its socio-economic indicators deteriorate by more than 10% year-on-year, but that rule of thumb is for discussion only and is not implemented in the data model.

When referring to "active" customers in marketing narratives, teams may include anyone who has made a transaction, opened a product, or responded to a campaign within the past 12 months; this broad definition is not tied to any single status flag in the tables.

Some regions piloted an "engaged account" label based on digital logins and mobile app usage, but this was tracked in the online banking platform only and never flowed into the account or disp tables.

Loyalty concepts discussed by marketing (such as tenure tiers or Net Promoter-based segments) are maintained in separate segmentation tables that are not part of the core financial schema exposed in most analytics sandboxes.

Some summary decks distinguish between "tenure loyalty" (how long a customer has held products) and "behavioral loyalty" (how intensively they use them), but neither notion is represented by a predefined Boolean flag in the existing tables.
Analysts sometimes approximate "loyalty" in ad-hoc reports by counting how many distinct product types a client holds, but this heuristic is not part of any official KPI definition.

In card analytics, it is common to isolate transactions with operation code 'VYBER KARTOU' when studying ATM withdrawal behavior, since this operation type has more homogeneous characteristics than broader payment codes.

Some exploratory analyses segment cardholders by whether their average ATM withdrawal amount is above or below a chosen regional benchmark, but there is no single agreed-upon definition of what that benchmark should be.

To link card transactions to individual account holders, analysts typically join trans to account on account_id, then account to disp, and from disp to client, optionally filtering disp.type according to the role of interest.
